frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
generated_comment:
Ali: Yeh jo woh kar rahi hai — hijab utaarna gate pe — yeh sirf kapde ki baat nahi hai. Yeh toh poori community ke saamne humara muh kaala kar rahi hai. Kal koi dekh le, koi jaanpehchaan wala, toh log kya kahenge? Hamari izzat uske haath mein hai, aur woh khel rahi hai usse. Sana ko samajhna hoga ki woh sirf apni beti nahi hai — woh ek Muslim ghar ki beti hai, aur uska har qadam hamare ghar ka, hamare mazhab ka, hamare mohalle ka aks hai.

Ali's wife: Lekin shayad hum thoda baat karein pehle, samjhein kyun woh aisa kar rahi hai—

Ali: Kyun? Kyun poochhna? Haq nahi hai usse aisa karne ka, bas. Kal se main khud chorr ke aaunga usse college. Dekhte hain phir kaise utarti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment frames the daughter's body and dress as an extension of family and community honour rather than her personal autonomy, encoding the 'body as community property' trope. The misogyny is covert because it is expressed through the language of religious duty and collective izzat rather than explicit denigration, yet it systematically subordinates her individual agency to male-family surveillance and control.
persona_text: You are Ali. Age 48. Married. A Muslim man in Hyderabad. Your 20 year old daughter Sana has started college this year. She has been wearing a hijab since she was 16 Ñ your family is conservative and this has always been understood. Last week you found out from your wife that Sana has been removing her hijab once she reaches college and putting it back on before coming home. She does not know you know. You are sitting with your wife tonight deciding what to say to Sana tomorrow and what this behaviour means and what the consequences should be and what a Muslim girl who removes her hijab at the college gate is telling her family and her community about who she is choosing to be.
